title: Alterations in Conformations of Poly(3-hexylthiophene) on Au(111) Induced by Annealing
authors: Anmol Arya, François Vonau, Solomon L. Joseph, Thomas Pfohl, Silvia Siegenführ, Laurent Simon, Günter Reiter
abstract: Employing high-vacuum electrospray deposition and scanning tunneling microscopy, we investigated how individual poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) chains navigated on the periodic energy landscape of a reconstructed Au(111) surface. The resulting polymer conformations were governed by the interplay between the periodically corrugated substrate, in particular the depth and regularity of the modulated surface potential, and thermal energy. On a regularly reconstructed surface, annealing at °C provided sufficient energy for chain segments to overcome energy barriers of the corrugated surface potential landscape, allowing monomers along the chain to experience a strong thermodynamic driving force toward the low-energy valleys on the surface. The adsorbed polymers adopted a state where the polymer conformations were replicating the herringbone pattern. By contrast, on an irregularly reconstructed surface, the correspondingly disordered potential landscape yielded a diverse mix of coiled polymer chains performing a two-dimensional random walk and collapsed chains located in troughs of the energy landscape. Intriguingly, annealing at °C forced polymers to form clusters of many chains. Our results establish that thermal energy and substrate topography represent control parameters for altering polymer conformations, providing a mechanistic framework for rationally designing polymer nanostructures at the molecular level.
summary: This paper investigates how the physical shape of polymer chains can be manipulated by controlling the underlying gold surface pattern and temperature. By using high-resolution STM, the authors show that a regular gold surface can act as a template to align polymers, whereas heat can be used to either move chains into surface valleys or force them to clump into large clusters. This provides a mechanistic way to design molecular-scale structures for organic electronics.
main_problem: Understanding how the conformation and self-organization of P3HT polymer chains on Au(111) are controlled by the interplay between substrate topography (periodic vs. irregular reconstruction) and thermal energy.
main_result: Annealing at 100 °C on regular Au(111) templates chains to follow the herringbone pattern, while 200 °C annealing induces large-scale chain clustering driven by pi-pi interactions; irregular surfaces instead lead to random-walk or collapsed states.
method: High-vacuum electrospray deposition followed by variable-temperature Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) and molecular modeling using Avogadro and LMAPper.
model_or_system: The system consists of regioregular poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) chains deposited on Au(111) single crystals featuring either periodic herringbone reconstruction or irregular surface states, modeled using the de Gennes 'adsorption blob' concept.
key_observables: Polymer conformations (random walk, coiled, collapsed, or corrugation-following), cluster area, cluster number density, and monomer-resolved dimensions.
important_parameters: Thermal energy (kBT), surface corrugation energy (Ec), corrugation width (D), and annealing temperature (100 °C and 200 °C).
main_assumptions: The polymer is treated as a sequence of adsorption blobs where thermal energy dominates within a blob and substrate potential dominates at larger scales.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of polymer adsorption on flat vs. corrugated surfaces; Figure 2 shows P3HT alignment on regular Au(111); Figure 3 shows random-walk and collapsed states on irregular Au(111); Figure 4 shows large multi-chain clusters formed after 200 °C annealing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of polymer conformation control, describes the experimental setup (electrospray and STM), details the energy landscape of the Au(111) substrate, presents results for regular vs. irregular surfaces under different annealing temperatures, and concludes with design principles for polymer nanostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic representation of a polymer chain confined on a corrugated surface. (a) As a reference, a polymer chain on a flat equipotential surface with a sequence of adsorption blobs of size 𝜉 and monomer size 𝑏. (b) 1D representation of the surface potential 𝑈(𝑥) (the red curve denotes a corrugated surface potential, while the gray line represents an equipotential surface). D is the corrugation width along the 𝑥 axis, and 𝐸c is the energy difference between peak and trough of the surface potential. (c) A polymer chain, represented as a sequence of adsorption blobs, on a periodically corrugated surface potential 𝑈(𝑥, 𝑦), which only partially follows the corrugations. (d) A polymer...

Figure 2: Influence of Au (111) corrugation on polymer aggregation. (a) Large-scale STM topography image on Au(R) after electrospray deposition of P3HT, showing that polymer chains followed the herringbone corrugation of Au (111). The size of the image (a) is 62 × 169 nm2 measured at a setpoint of 10 pA and a bias voltage of − 2.55 V. (b) STM topography image (42 × 42 nm2) of Au (111) before electrospray deposition of P3HT (measured at a setpoint of 1.7 nA and a bias voltage of – 0.2 V), showing the herringbone reconstruction. In the inset, we superimposed a smaller scale topography image (23 × 23 nm2) of P3HT chains deposited on the corrugated surface (measured at a setpoint of 10 pA and a...

Figure 3: Influence of irregular corrugations on an Au(111) surface (Au(IRR)) on polymer conformations. (a) STM topography image of Au (111) with an irregular surface reconstruction before electrospray deposition. (b) Large-scale STM topography image on Au(IRR) after P3HT deposition, showing polymer chains in a 2D random conformations (white circle, see also (c)) and a collapsed state (red circle, see also (d)). The area of image (a) is 60 × 60 nm 2, measured at bias of −1.5 V and a setpoint of 300 pA, (b) of area: 60 × 60 nm2 at bias: −1.51 V, setpoint: 5 pA, (c) of area 23 × 23 nm2 at setpoint of 10 pA, bias voltage of 2.0 V, and (d) of area: 10 × 10 nm2 at bias: −1.51 V, setpoint: 5 pA.

Figure 4: Thermal annealing resulted in aggregation of multiple P3HT chains. (a) STM topography image showing clusters consisting of multiple P3HT chains deposited on Au(R) after P3HT and annealed at 200 °C. The scale bar of 50 nm applies also to the inset, which reproduces Figure 2 (a), i.e., a large- scale STM topography image showing P3HT chains after annealing to 100 °C, which followed the herringbone reconstruction of Au(R). (b, c, d) Smaller scale images showing individual clusters of P3HT chains. Image (a) has a size of 269 × 269 nm² and was acquired with a setpoint of 120 pA and a bias voltage of – 1.3 V.
